$comment: Potato Service Knowledge Base. Edit this file, then run `node _generate_kb.js` to splice it into index.html. Each entry has the 4 high-value fields: limits, whenToUse, cost, pitfalls plus an OPTIONAL costEstimate { small, medium, large } used by the Cost Estimator. The `matches` array drives runtime lookup against a node's type/category. Update the asOf date in cost strings when refreshing.

lambda.name: AWS Lambda
lambda.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region (Multi-AZ): 99.95% | Multi-Region: 99.999975% (calculated)
lambda.limits: Max execution: 15 min timeout | Memory: 128 MB–10 GB | Payload: 6 MB sync / 256 KB async | Cold start: 100ms–1s | Concurrency: 1,000 default (soft limit) | Deployment pkg: 50 MB zipped / 250 MB unzipped | /tmp storage: 512 MB–10 GB
lambda.whenToUse: Best for short-lived, event-driven tasks. Scales to zero — no idle cost. Ideal for API handlers, S3 triggers, scheduled jobs under 15 min.
lambda.cost: $0.20 per 1M requests + $0.0000166 per GB-second. Free tier: 1M requests + 400k GB-s/mo forever. ARM (Graviton) is ~20% cheaper than x86. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: VPC cold starts add 1-5s — keep Lambda outside VPC unless you need private resources
Concurrency soft-cap of 1,000 — request limit increase early for production workloads
/tmp is 512 MB by default, ephemeral — provision more if processing large files
Pay for the full GB-s allocation, not actual usage — over-provisioning memory is expensive

ec2.name: Amazon EC2
ec2.sla: Single AZ (single instance): 99.5% | Single Region (Multi-AZ): 99.99% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
ec2.limits: No execution timeout (runs indefinitely) | Requires manual scaling or ASG | EBS I/O depends on instance type | Instance startup: 30–90s | No built-in HA without ALB + ASG
ec2.whenToUse: Best for long-running workloads, stateful apps, or when you need full OS control. Use when Lambda timeout or memory limits are a constraint.
ec2.cost: On-demand t3.medium ~$30/mo. Reserved 1yr saves ~40%, 3yr saves ~62%. Spot saves 70-90% but can be interrupted. Data egress: $0.09/GB to internet. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Stopped instances still charge for attached EBS — terminate to fully stop billing
NAT Gateway for private subnet egress costs ~$32/mo + $0.045/GB — often the surprise bill
Cross-AZ data transfer is $0.01/GB each way — pin chatty services to one AZ
Default instance limits per region are low — request limit increases before launching at scale

ecs.name: Amazon ECS
ecs.sla: Single AZ (single task): 99.0% | Single Region (Multi-AZ): 99.99% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
ecs.limits: Task CPU: 0.25–16 vCPU | Task memory: 512 MB–120 GB | Max containers per task: 10 | Service startup: ~30–60s | No automatic rollback without CodeDeploy
ecs.whenToUse: Best for containerised microservices needing persistent processes. Easier ops than raw EC2, more flexible than Lambda for long-running workloads.
ecs.cost: EC2 launch type: pay for EC2 instances + EBS (no ECS premium). Fargate launch type: $0.04048/vCPU-hour + $0.004445/GB-hour. ARM Fargate ~20% cheaper. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Fargate has no Spot option for older platform versions — check before assuming Spot savings
Task definitions are immutable — every change creates a new revision (old revisions accrue)
Service Auto Scaling default cooldowns are slow — tune for traffic patterns
Container insights metrics are NOT free — opt-in adds CloudWatch costs

eks.name: Amazon EKS
eks.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Standard Control Plane: 99.95% | Provisioned Control Plane: 99.99% | Multi-Region: 99.999975% (calculated)
eks.limits: Max pods per node: 110 (t3.medium) to 750 (large) | Max nodes per cluster: 5,000 | Control plane SLA: 99.95% | Add-on updates require manual intervention | Higher ops overhead than ECS
eks.whenToUse: Best when you need full Kubernetes compatibility, multi-cloud portability, or advanced scheduling. Higher complexity than ECS — justify with scale or K8s-specific tooling.
eks.cost: Control plane: $0.10/hour per cluster (~$73/mo). Plus EC2/Fargate for nodes. EKS Auto Mode adds $0.0029/vCPU-hr for AWS-managed nodes. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Control plane charge is per-cluster — running dev/staging/prod = 3x the base cost
AWS Load Balancer Controller pods cost extra and must be in place before ingress works
Cluster Autoscaler vs Karpenter — Karpenter is faster and cheaper for most workloads now
Kubernetes version upgrades every ~3 months, AWS supports each version for ~14 months

fargate.name: AWS Fargate
fargate.sla: Single AZ (single task): 99.0% | Single Region (Multi-AZ): 99.99% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
fargate.limits: Max CPU: 16 vCPU | Max memory: 120 GB | No persistent local storage (use EFS) | Cold start: 30–60s | Cannot access EC2 instance metadata
fargate.whenToUse: Serverless containers — no EC2 management. Pay per task second. Best for unpredictable or bursty container workloads.
fargate.cost: $0.04048/vCPU-hour + $0.004445/GB-hour (x86). ARM ~20% cheaper. Spot ~70% off. Billed per second, 1-min minimum. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Cold starts are 30-60s — not suitable for latency-sensitive scale-up
No daemonset equivalent — each task gets its own networking
Ephemeral storage is 20 GB default; charged extra above that
Cheaper than EC2 only for low-utilization workloads — high-utilization EC2 Reserved wins

batch.name: AWS Batch
batch.limits: Job timeout: configurable, no hard limit | Max job queues: 50 | Max compute environments: 50 | Spot interruption risk if using Spot fleet | Job arrays: max 10,000 jobs
batch.whenToUse: Best for large-scale batch processing (ML training, ETL, rendering). Automatically manages EC2/Spot fleet. Cost-efficient for non-time-sensitive workloads.
batch.cost: No charge for the Batch service itself. Pay for underlying EC2 / Fargate / Spot. Spot fleet typically 70-90% off on-demand. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Spot interruption can kill long jobs — use checkpointing or non-Spot for critical workloads
Job array indices are limited to 10,000 — partition very large workloads
Compute environment scaling has lag — provision generously for time-sensitive batches
batch.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: no published service SLA — Batch is free; SLA applies to underlying EC2/Fargate (99.99%) | Multi-Region: derive from compute SLA

s3.name: Amazon S3
s3.sla: Single AZ: N/A (S3 is regional) | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
s3.limits: Max object size: 5 TB | Single PUT: 5 GB (use multipart above 100 MB) | Max bucket count: 100 per account (soft) | No native query — use Athena/S3 Select | S3 Select: 128 MB max | Lifecycle rules: 1,000 per bucket | Eventual consistency: READ-after-DELETE may return old data
s3.whenToUse: Industry-standard object store. 11 nines durability. Best for files, backups, static assets, data lake landing zone. Not suitable for transactional or low-latency random access.
s3.cost: Standard: $0.023/GB/mo for first 50 TB. PUT: $0.005/1k requests. GET: $0.0004/1k. Data egress: $0.09/GB to internet. Intelligent-Tiering auto-saves 70%+ on cold data. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Data egress is the single biggest S3 surprise bill — pair with CloudFront for served content
Lifecycle policies don't apply retroactively to existing objects without a transition rule
Versioning + lifecycle requires careful policy design or versioned objects multiply storage
Cross-region replication doubles storage cost AND adds egress charges

ebs.name: Amazon EBS
ebs.sla: Single AZ: 99.999% (durability, not uptime SLA) | Multi-AZ: N/A (AZ-bound) | Multi-Region: no official SLA
ebs.limits: Max volume size: 64 TB (io2) | Max IOPS: 256,000 (io2 Block Express) | Single-AZ only — not HA across AZs | Attach to one EC2 at a time (except io1/io2 Multi-Attach, max 16) | Snapshots are eventually consistent
ebs.whenToUse: Block storage for EC2. Best for databases, OS volumes, high-IOPS workloads needing persistence. Tied to one AZ — design failover accordingly.
ebs.cost: gp3: $0.08/GB/mo (3,000 IOPS + 125 MB/s included). io2 Block Express: $0.125/GB + $0.065/IOPS. Snapshots: $0.05/GB/mo. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Detached volumes still bill — track them with AWS Config or Trusted Advisor
Snapshots are incremental but billing is for blocks held — old snapshots stack up
gp3 is almost always cheaper than gp2 — actively migrate
Cross-AZ failover requires snapshot + restore — not instant

efs.name: Amazon EFS
efs.sla: Single AZ: no formal SLA | Single Region (Multi-AZ): 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
efs.limits: Throughput: Bursting (baseline 1 MB/s per GB) or Provisioned | Latency: ~1–3ms (higher than EBS) | Max file size: 52 TB | Max files per filesystem: unlimited | Costs ~3x EBS for same storage
efs.whenToUse: Shared NFS file system — multiple EC2/ECS/Lambda can mount simultaneously. Best for shared config, media processing pipelines, CMS workloads.
efs.cost: Standard storage: $0.30/GB/mo. Infrequent Access: $0.025/GB + $0.01/GB per read. One Zone tier: 47% cheaper but single-AZ. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: ~3x more expensive than EBS for same GB — only use when sharing is actually needed
Bursting throughput depletes credits with sustained I/O — switch to Provisioned for steady workloads
Lifecycle to IA can cause unexpected read charges if access pattern is wrong

glacier.name: Amazon S3 Glacier
glacier.sla: Single AZ: N/A (S3 is regional) | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
glacier.limits: Retrieval time: Expedited 1–5 min ($$$), Standard 3–5 hrs, Bulk 5–12 hrs | Min storage duration: 90 days (Glacier), 180 days (Deep Archive) | Max archive size: 40 TB | Not suitable for real-time access
glacier.whenToUse: Lowest-cost AWS storage. Best for compliance archiving, long-term backups, regulatory data retention. Plan retrieval times into your recovery objectives.
glacier.cost: Glacier Flexible: $0.0036/GB/mo. Glacier Deep Archive: $0.00099/GB/mo (cheapest cloud storage). Retrieval fees: $0.01-$0.10/GB depending on speed. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Early deletion charges if removed before minimum duration (90/180 days)
Expedited retrieval is expensive — budget for retrieval, not just storage
Glacier Instant Retrieval is different — same retrieval speed as S3 Standard, higher storage cost

rds.name: Amazon RDS
rds.sla: Single AZ (single instance): 99.5% | Single Region (Multi-AZ): 99.95% | Multi-Region: 99.999975% (calculated)
rds.limits: Max DB size: 64 TB (Aurora) / 16–32 TB (others) | Max connections: depends on instance RAM (e.g. db.t3.medium ≈ 60 connections) | Read replicas: max 15 (Aurora) / 5 (MySQL/Postgres) | Failover time: 60–120s | No horizontal write scaling — single primary writer
rds.whenToUse: Best for relational, ACID-compliant workloads. Managed patching, backups, Multi-AZ HA. Use Aurora for better performance and auto-scaling storage.
rds.cost: On-demand db.t3.medium MySQL: ~$60/mo + storage. Multi-AZ doubles compute cost. Reserved 1yr saves ~40%. Backups: free up to allocated storage size. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Storage can only grow, never shrink — overprovisioning is permanent
Multi-AZ standby is NOT a read replica — it's idle until failover
Engine version upgrades require downtime — schedule maintenance windows carefully
Reserved instances are tied to instance type — wrong size = wasted commitment

dynamo.name: Amazon DynamoDB
dynamo.sla: Single AZ: N/A (always multi-AZ within region) | Single Region: 99.99% | Multi-Region (Global Tables): 99.999% (AWS-committed)
dynamo.limits: Max item size: 400 KB | Max partition key throughput: 3,000 RCU / 1,000 WCU per partition | Query must use partition key — cannot do full table scans efficiently | GSI propagation: eventual consistency | Max GSIs: 20 per table | BatchWriteItem: max 25 items | Transactions: max 100 items / 4 MB
dynamo.whenToUse: Best for high-throughput key-value or document access at any scale. Sub-millisecond latency. Avoid for complex queries, joins, or aggregations — use Athena or Aurora instead.
dynamo.cost: On-demand: $1.25/M write requests + $0.25/M read requests + $0.25/GB/mo storage. Provisioned: $0.00065/WCU-hr + $0.00013/RCU-hr. Standard-IA tier saves ~60% on rarely-read data. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Scans cost the same as full reads — design access patterns around partition key from day one
Hot partitions cap at 3,000 RCU / 1,000 WCU regardless of table provisioning
GSI propagation lag (~100ms) makes them unsuitable for read-after-write consistency
Backup costs (PITR + on-demand) often exceed table storage costs
Item size limit of 400 KB — large items overflow into S3 pointer pattern

elasticache.name: Amazon ElastiCache
elasticache.sla: Single AZ: no formal SLA | Single Region (Multi-AZ or Serverless): 99.99% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
elasticache.limits: Redis max memory: node-type dependent (e.g. r6g.large = 13 GB) | Redis Cluster max nodes: 500 | Memcached max nodes: 300 | No persistence in Memcached | Redis AOF persistence adds latency | Max key size: 512 MB | Max value: 512 MB
elasticache.whenToUse: In-memory cache for sub-millisecond reads. Best for session storage, leaderboards, rate limiting, DB query caching. Use Redis for persistence/pub-sub, Memcached for pure caching.
elasticache.cost: cache.t4g.micro Redis: ~$12/mo. cache.r6g.large: ~$185/mo. Reserved 1yr saves ~40%. Serverless: $0.125/GB-hr storage + $0.0034/ECU. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Multi-AZ replication doubles cost — confirm RPO/RTO needs justify it
Cluster mode enabled requires app-side awareness (CRC16 slot routing)
Backups (snapshots) charge separately — set sensible retention
Reserved nodes are tied to specific node type — wrong choice = stuck

aurora.name: Amazon Aurora
aurora.sla: Single AZ: 99.9% | Single Region (Multi-AZ): 99.99% | Multi-Region (Global Database): 99.99%+ (no separate AWS-committed global SLA)
aurora.limits: Max storage: 128 TB (auto-scales) | Max read replicas: 15 | Failover: ~30s | Aurora Serverless v2 min: 0.5 ACU | Max connections: instance-dependent | Writer endpoint is single — no multi-writer by default (except Aurora Global)
aurora.whenToUse: MySQL/Postgres compatible with up to 5x performance. Auto-scaling storage, 6-way replication, fast failover. Best when RDS limits are hit or high availability is critical.
aurora.cost: db.r6g.large: ~$210/mo. Storage: $0.10/GB/mo. I/O: $0.20/M requests. Aurora Serverless v2: $0.12/ACU-hr (~$87/mo for 1 ACU 24/7). (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: I/O charges can dominate cost for write-heavy workloads — consider I/O-Optimized cluster ($0.225/GB but no I/O charge)
Serverless v2 minimum 0.5 ACU bills 24/7 — not truly serverless for idle workloads
Aurora Global is expensive (~2x storage) — worth it only for cross-region DR
Cluster volume can't be downsized once grown

redshift.name: Amazon Redshift
redshift.sla: Single AZ single-node: 99.5% | Single AZ multi-node / Serverless: 99.9% | Multi-AZ cluster: 99.99% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
redshift.limits: Max table size: no hard limit but query perf degrades past billions of rows without tuning | Sort keys required for range queries | Max columns per table: 1,600 | COPY is fastest load method — INSERT row-by-row is very slow | Concurrency: max 50 WLM queries | VACUUM needed after large deletes
redshift.whenToUse: Columnar analytical database for petabyte-scale OLAP queries. Best for BI dashboards, data warehousing. Not for OLTP — use RDS/Aurora for transactional workloads.
redshift.cost: ra3.xlplus: ~$1.09/hr (~$795/mo). Storage (RMS): $0.024/GB/mo. Reserved 1yr saves ~25%, 3yr saves ~75%. Serverless: $0.36/RPU-hour, 60s minimum. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Concurrency Scaling clusters charge separately if peak exceeds main cluster
Sort key + dist key design is critical and hard to change later
Spectrum (S3 query) charges $5/TB scanned — partition + Parquet to control cost
Pause/resume saves compute cost but storage still bills

vpc.name: Amazon VPC
vpc.sla: Single AZ: no SLA | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
vpc.limits: Max VPCs per region: 5 (soft) | Max subnets per VPC: 200 | Max security groups per ENI: 5 | Max rules per SG: 60 inbound + 60 outbound | VPC peering: non-transitive — A↔B, B↔C does not mean A↔C | Max route table entries: 1,000
vpc.whenToUse: Isolated network boundary for your AWS resources. Use private subnets for databases/Lambda, public subnets for ALB/NAT. Design CIDR ranges for future peering needs.
vpc.cost: VPC itself: free. NAT Gateway: ~$32/mo + $0.045/GB processed. VPC Endpoints (Interface): $0.01/hr + $0.01/GB. PrivateLink: $0.01/hr + data. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: NAT Gateway is the #1 surprise bill — one per AZ + data processing adds up fast
CIDR ranges can't be expanded, only added — plan for 5-10x growth on day one
Default VPCs in every region cost nothing but waste IP space — delete unused
Cross-AZ data transfer is $0.01/GB each way even inside a VPC

alb.name: Application Load Balancer
alb.sla: Single AZ (single LB): 99.5% | Single Region (Multi-AZ): 99.99% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
alb.limits: Max target groups: 100 | Max rules per listener: 100 | Max targets per group: 1,000 | SSL cert limit: 25 per ALB | WebSocket support: yes | gRPC: yes | Sticky sessions: yes (cookie-based) | Not suitable for TCP/UDP — use NLB
alb.whenToUse: Layer 7 HTTP/HTTPS load balancer. Routes by path, host, headers. Best for web apps, microservices, API routing. Pair with ECS/EKS/EC2 target groups.
alb.cost: $0.0225/hr (~$16/mo) + $0.008/LCU-hr. Free SSL via ACM. NLB is similar pricing structure for L4. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: LCU charges scale with new connections, active connections, processed bytes, and rule evaluations
Idle ALBs still bill the hourly base — delete unused dev/staging LBs
Cross-zone load balancing is enabled by default but adds inter-AZ data transfer cost

cloudfront.name: Amazon CloudFront
cloudfront.sla: Single AZ: N/A (global edge service) | Global: 99.9% | Multi-Region: N/A (already global by design)
cloudfront.limits: Max distributions: 200 per account | Cache TTL: 0–31,536,000s | Max object size: 20 GB | Custom origin timeout: max 60s | Functions max execution: 2ms (CloudFront Functions) / 30s (Lambda@Edge) | Lambda@Edge cold start adds 100–500ms
cloudfront.whenToUse: Global CDN with 400+ edge locations. Reduces latency for static/dynamic content. Best for S3 static sites, API caching, WAF integration. Configure cache policies carefully to avoid stale data.
cloudfront.cost: Data out to internet: $0.085/GB (US/EU), tiered down to $0.02/GB at PB scale. HTTPS requests: $0.0075-$0.0120/10k. Free tier: 1 TB out + 10M requests/mo forever. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Origin requests still cost S3 GET fees — high cache miss rate doubles your bill
Lambda@Edge runs at every edge region — debugging is hard, costs add up
Invalidations: first 1000/mo free, then $0.005 each — use versioned filenames instead
WAF on CloudFront is in us-east-1 only — region-specific quirk

route53.name: Amazon Route 53
route53.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Global: 100% (AWS-committed) | Multi-Region: N/A (already global)
route53.limits: Max hosted zones: 500 per account | Max records per zone: 10,000 | Health check interval: min 10s (standard) / 30s (basic) | DNS TTL affects failover speed — low TTL = faster failover but more queries | Max routing policies: Latency, Weighted, Geolocation, Failover, Multi-value
route53.whenToUse: AWS-native DNS with health checks, routing policies, and domain management. Best for multi-region failover, blue/green deployments, and latency-based routing.
route53.cost: $0.50/mo per hosted zone (first 25) + $0.40 per 1M queries. Health checks: $0.50/mo per check. Domain registration varies. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Health check costs surprise people — $0.50/mo × dozens of endpoints = real money
TTL changes don't propagate until existing TTL expires — plan migrations days ahead
Alias records to AWS resources are free queries — use them over CNAME where possible

apigateway.name: Amazon API Gateway
apigateway.sla: Single AZ: N/A (regional service) | Single Region: 99.95% | Multi-Region: 99.999975% (calculated)
apigateway.limits: Max timeout: 29s (REST) / 29s (HTTP) / no limit (WebSocket) | Max payload: 10 MB (REST) / 10 MB (HTTP) | Throttling: 10,000 RPS default (account-wide soft limit) | Max stages: 10 per API | Caching: 0.5 GB–237 GB, adds latency on misses | Max integrations per API: 300
apigateway.whenToUse: Managed API front door for Lambda, HTTP backends, AWS services. Best for REST/WebSocket APIs with auth, throttling, caching built-in. Use HTTP API for lower cost/latency, REST API for full features.
apigateway.cost: REST API: $3.50/M calls. HTTP API: $1.00/M calls (much cheaper for simple Lambda proxy). WebSocket: $1.00/M messages + $0.25/M connection-min. Cache: $0.02/hr per GB. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: REST API is 3.5x more expensive than HTTP API — only pay for it if you need features like usage plans or full request transforms
Caching has a flat hourly cost regardless of utilization — measure cache hit rate
29s timeout is hard — long requests need WebSocket or async patterns
Authorizers and custom domains are billed extra

waf.name: AWS WAF
waf.limits: Max Web ACLs: 100 per account | Max rules per ACL: 100 | Max rule groups: 100 | WCU limit: 1,500 per ACL | Managed rules count toward WCU | Regex patterns: max 10 per set | Logging: must configure separately to Kinesis/S3
waf.whenToUse: Layer 7 firewall — SQL injection, XSS, bot protection, IP blocking. Best placed in front of CloudFront or ALB. Use AWS Managed Rules for quick baseline protection.
waf.cost: $5/mo per Web ACL + $1/mo per rule + $0.60/M requests. Managed rule groups: $1-$10/mo each + per-request fees. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Per-request charge applies to ALL inspected traffic, including blocked — bots can drive up cost
Managed rule groups each cost separately — stack them carefully
WAF logs go to Kinesis/S3/CloudWatch but storage is your bill — set retention
waf.sla: Single AZ: N/A (global service) | Single Region: 99.95% | Multi-Region: 99.999975% (calculated)

bedrock.name: Amazon Bedrock
bedrock.limits: RPM/TPM limits per model vary — Claude Sonnet: ~500 RPM default | Max context: model-dependent (Claude Sonnet 4.6: 200K tokens) | No training — inference only | Cross-region inference available | On-demand pricing — no reserved capacity | Knowledge base chunking: max 10,000 chars
bedrock.whenToUse: Managed foundation model API — no infrastructure to manage. Best for generative AI features, RAG pipelines, agent orchestration. Choose model based on latency vs capability trade-off.
bedrock.cost: Claude Sonnet 4.6: $3/M input tokens + $15/M output tokens. Claude Haiku 4.5: $1/M in + $5/M out. Provisioned Throughput: hourly commitment for guaranteed capacity. Prompt caching saves up to 90% on cached prefix. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Output tokens cost 3-5x input tokens — verbose prompts/responses double the bill fast
RPM/TPM quotas are low by default — request increases early for production
Knowledge Base storage uses OpenSearch Serverless underneath — that's a separate, significant bill
Cross-region inference adds latency and inter-region data transfer cost
bedrock.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

sagemaker.name: Amazon SageMaker
sagemaker.limits: Training job max runtime: 5 days | Max parallel training jobs: 20 (soft) | Endpoint auto-scaling: min 30s scale-out | Real-time inference timeout: 60s | Batch transform: no timeout limit | Max model size for real-time: instance memory dependent | Feature store: eventual consistency for online store
sagemaker.whenToUse: End-to-end ML platform — training, hosting, pipelines. Best when you need custom model training or fine-tuning. Heavier ops than Bedrock — justify with custom model requirements.
sagemaker.cost: Endpoint instance hours + storage. ml.m5.large: ~$0.115/hr (~$84/mo 24/7). Studio notebooks: same instance pricing. Training: pay per second of training instance. Savings Plans available. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Endpoints run 24/7 once deployed — even with zero traffic — delete or use Serverless Inference
Studio notebooks left running overnight burn money — auto-shutdown extensions are essential
Multi-model endpoints save cost but cold-load latency varies
Feature Store online and offline stores have separate pricing
sagemaker.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.95% (real-time inference endpoints) / 99.9% (training jobs, notebooks) | Multi-Region: 99.999975% (calculated)

rekognition.name: Amazon Rekognition
rekognition.limits: Max image size: 5 MB (API) / 15 MB (S3) | Max faces per collection: 20 million | DetectLabels: max 100 labels | Video analysis is async — polling required | Not real-time for video (use Kinesis Video Streams) | PPE detection accuracy varies by environment
rekognition.whenToUse: Managed computer vision — face detection, object labels, text extraction, content moderation. Best for image/video analysis without ML expertise. Async for video, sync for images.
rekognition.cost: Image analysis: $1.00 per 1,000 images for first 1M. Face search: $1.00 per 1,000 face vectors. Video: $0.10 per minute analyzed. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Face collections charge for storage + each search — set TTL on collections
Custom Labels models charge by training and inference hours, NOT per call
Video analysis is async — your code must poll or subscribe to SNS for completion
rekognition.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

textract.name: Amazon Textract
textract.limits: Max document size: 500 MB (S3) / 10 MB (API) | Async API for multi-page PDFs | Max pages per async job: 3,000 | Queries per page: max 15 | Forms/tables accuracy: high for structured docs, lower for handwriting | Not suitable for scanned images with <200 DPI
textract.whenToUse: Managed OCR + document understanding. Extracts text, forms, tables from PDFs/images. Best for document processing pipelines (invoices, contracts, ID verification).
textract.cost: DetectDocumentText: $1.50 per 1,000 pages. AnalyzeDocument (forms+tables): $50 per 1,000 pages. Queries: $15 per 1,000 pages. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: AnalyzeDocument is 33x more expensive than DetectDocumentText — only use when you need structure
Async jobs charge by completion — abandoned jobs still bill if they finish
Low-DPI scans accuracy drops rapidly — pre-process inputs if quality varies
textract.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

comprehend.name: Amazon Comprehend
comprehend.limits: Max document size: 100 KB (sync) / 5 GB (async batch) | Language detection: 100 languages | Entity recognition latency: ~100ms | Custom classifier training: requires 1,000+ examples per label | Async batch jobs: max 5 concurrent
comprehend.whenToUse: NLP service — sentiment, entities, key phrases, language detection, topic modelling. Best for analysing large text corpora, customer feedback, support tickets.
comprehend.cost: $0.0001 per 100 characters for built-in features. Custom models: training + inference billed separately. PII detection: $0.0001 per 100 chars. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Billed per 100 characters, so verbose text amplifies cost
Custom classifier needs 1,000+ training examples per label — gather data before committing
Bedrock with Claude is often cheaper AND more accurate for general NLP now
comprehend.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

iam.name: AWS IAM
iam.limits: Max managed policies per account: 1,500 | Max policy size: 6,144 chars (inline) / 6,144 chars (managed) | Max roles per account: 1,000 | Session duration: 1hr (default) to 12hrs | Permission boundaries do not grant permissions — only restrict | Max policy versions: 5
iam.whenToUse: Foundation of AWS security. Follow least-privilege principle. Use roles for EC2/Lambda (not access keys). Enable MFA for all human users. Audit with IAM Access Analyzer.
iam.cost: Free. IAM Access Analyzer: free for external access findings, charged for unused-access findings ($0.20 per IAM role analyzed). (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Wildcards in policies (*) are the #1 source of breaches — always scope to resource ARNs
Long-lived access keys are dangerous — rotate or use IAM Identity Center / roles instead
Default session duration is 1hr — assume role expiry can break batch jobs
Permission boundaries restrict, never grant — separate from policies attached
iam.sla: Single AZ: N/A (global control plane) | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

cognito.name: Amazon Cognito
cognito.limits: User pool: max 40 million users | App clients per user pool: 300 | Custom attributes: 25 per user pool | Token validity: Access 5min–1day, ID 5min–1day, Refresh 1hr–10yrs | Email sending limit: 50/day free tier, SES for more | Lambda triggers: max 5s execution
cognito.whenToUse: Managed user auth — sign-up, sign-in, MFA, social federation. Best for web/mobile app auth. Avoid for M2M — use IAM roles or Client Credentials OAuth instead.
cognito.cost: Free tier: 10,000 MAUs (50,000 for SAML/OIDC). Above: $0.0055/MAU (sliding scale down to $0.0025). SMS MFA charged separately. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: SMS MFA is expensive in some countries — TOTP MFA is free
User Pool deletion is permanent — back up users before testing schema migrations
Email sending defaults to 50/day — switch to SES early for any real volume
Migration from another IdP requires Lambda trigger gymnastics
cognito.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% (User Pools) | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

kms.name: AWS KMS
kms.limits: API rate: 5,500–30,000 req/s per region depending on key type | Key rotation: automatic annual rotation for AWS-managed | CMK cost: $1/month per key | Max data encrypted per call: 4 KB (use envelope encryption for larger data) | Cross-account key sharing requires resource policy
kms.whenToUse: Managed encryption key service. Use envelope encryption — KMS encrypts your data key, data key encrypts data. Enables audit trail via CloudTrail. Required for many compliance frameworks.
kms.cost: $1/mo per customer-managed key. Requests: $0.03 per 10,000 (first 20k free/mo). AWS-managed keys are free. Custom Key Store: $1/hr per cluster + HSM costs. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Cross-region replication and aliases multiply key count — each is $1/mo
High-volume operations (Lambda decrypt-on-cold-start) can hit API rate limits — cache data keys
Deleting a key has a mandatory 7-30 day waiting period — plan deprecation
kms.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.999% (five nines — foundational service) | Multi-Region: 99.99999998% (calculated)

secrets.name: AWS Secrets Manager
secrets.limits: Max secret size: 65,536 bytes | Max secrets per account: 500,000 | Rotation Lambda must complete within 4hrs | API rate: 2,500 req/s (GetSecretValue) per region | Cross-region replication: yes | Cost: $0.40/month per secret + $0.05 per 10,000 API calls
secrets.whenToUse: Managed secrets storage with automatic rotation. Best for DB passwords, API keys, certs. Never hardcode secrets in code or env vars — use Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store.
secrets.cost: $0.40/mo per secret + $0.05 per 10k API calls. Cross-region replicated secrets bill per region. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Secrets x lots of microservices = thousands of $0.40 charges — SSM Parameter Store free tier is often enough
Rotation Lambda failures leave the secret in an inconsistent state — alert on rotation failures
Each region replica is a full $0.40/mo secret
secrets.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

shield.name: AWS Shield
shield.limits: Standard: free, auto for all AWS resources | Advanced: $3,000/month, covers EC2/ELB/CloudFront/Route53 | Advanced DDoS response team: 24/7 | L3/L4 protection: Standard | L7 protection: Advanced + WAF required | SLA for cost credits: must be Advanced
shield.whenToUse: DDoS protection. Standard is always-on and free. Advanced adds cost protection, attack visibility, and AWS DRT escalation. Required for public-facing critical infrastructure.
shield.cost: Standard: free. Advanced: $3,000/mo per organization + data transfer fees during attacks (DDoS Response Team consultation included). (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Advanced subscription requires a 1-year commitment — $36k minimum spend
WAF rules included in Advanced — don't double-pay for managed rule groups
Cost protection refunds scale-out costs during attacks, not steady-state
shield.sla: Single AZ: N/A (global) | Shield Standard: bundled, no separate SLA | Shield Advanced: 100% protection against listed attack types (commitment, not uptime) | Multi-Region: global

sqs.name: Amazon SQS
sqs.sla: Single AZ: N/A (regional service) | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
sqs.limits: Max message size: 256 KB (use S3 Extended Client for larger) | Max retention: 14 days | Visibility timeout: max 12hrs | Standard: at-least-once delivery, out-of-order | FIFO: exactly-once, ordered, max 3,000 msg/s (batching) | Max in-flight messages: 120,000 (Standard) / 20,000 (FIFO) | DLQ redrive: max 1,000 messages/s
sqs.whenToUse: Decouples producers from consumers. Use Standard for high throughput, FIFO for ordered processing. Best for async workloads, retry logic, load levelling. Pair with Lambda or ECS consumers.
sqs.cost: Standard: $0.40 per 1M requests (free tier: 1M/mo forever). FIFO: $0.50 per 1M. Long polling reduces empty receives. Each batch of 10 = 1 request. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Empty receives still count as requests — use long polling (WaitTimeSeconds=20)
FIFO message group ID determines parallelism — single group = single consumer
Visibility timeout too short = duplicate processing, too long = stuck messages
Always set up a DLQ — silently dropped messages are the worst failure mode

sns.name: Amazon SNS
sns.sla: Single AZ: N/A (regional service) | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
sns.limits: Max message size: 256 KB | Max subscriptions per topic: 12,500,000 | Max topics: 100,000 | FIFO topic: max 300 msg/s | Email delivery: not guaranteed (spam filters) | No message retention — fire and forget | SMS costs vary by country
sns.whenToUse: Fan-out pub/sub — one message to many subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email, SMS). Best for event broadcasting, alerts, decoupled notification systems.
sns.cost: $0.50 per 1M publishes. Delivery: $0.50/M to SQS, $0.60/M to Lambda, $1.00/M to email, SMS varies by country (often $0.01-$0.075 per message). (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: SMS to international numbers is expensive AND has unpredictable delivery rates
Email throttled to 50/day for sandbox accounts — request production access early
No message retention — failed deliveries lost without DLQ on each subscription

eventbridge.name: Amazon EventBridge
eventbridge.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.99% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
eventbridge.limits: Max rules per bus: 300 | Max targets per rule: 5 | Event size: max 256 KB | Invocation rate: 18,750/s per region | Event replay: max 24hr retention for replay | Schema registry: max 100 schemas | Cross-account: requires resource policy
eventbridge.whenToUse: Serverless event bus — routes events from AWS services, SaaS, custom apps. Best for event-driven architectures, replacing cron jobs (Scheduler), and cross-service decoupling.
eventbridge.cost: $1.00 per 1M custom events. AWS service events are free. Schema discovery: $0.10 per 1M events processed. EventBridge Scheduler: $1.00 per 1M invocations. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Cross-region replication via rules doubles event cost
Schema discovery on a busy bus is expensive — turn off after schemas stabilize
DLQ on each rule is essential — failed delivery is otherwise silent

stepfunctions.name: AWS Step Functions
stepfunctions.limits: Max state machine definition: 1 MB | Max execution history: 25,000 events | Execution duration: Standard max 1 year / Express max 5 min | Max concurrent executions: 2,000 (Standard, soft) | Map state: max 40 concurrent iterations | API Gateway integration: 29s timeout still applies
stepfunctions.whenToUse: Visual workflow orchestration — retries, parallel branches, human approval steps. Best for complex multi-step processes (order processing, ML pipelines). Use Express for high-volume short workflows.
stepfunctions.cost: Standard: $25 per 1M state transitions (very expensive for fine-grained workflows). Express: $1.00 per 1M requests + duration. Inline Map saves transitions. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Standard state machines bill per transition — chatty workflows cost a lot
Express is 25x cheaper for short workflows — convert when execution < 5 min
Execution history capped at 25k events — long-running workflows hit this fast
Hidden DescribeExecution polling from your code can rate-limit
stepfunctions.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% (Standard + Express) | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

kinesis.name: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
kinesis.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
kinesis.limits: Shard throughput: 1 MB/s write / 2 MB/s read per shard | Max record size: 1 MB | Retention: 24hrs default, max 365 days (extended) | GetRecords: max 10,000 records / 10 MB per call | Shard splitting takes ~30s | Consumer limit: 5 GetRecords calls/s per shard (use Enhanced Fan-Out for more)
kinesis.whenToUse: Real-time data streaming. Best for clickstreams, IoT telemetry, log aggregation, real-time analytics. Scale shards based on throughput. Use Enhanced Fan-Out for multiple low-latency consumers.
kinesis.cost: Provisioned: $0.015/shard-hour (~$11/shard-mo) + $0.014/M PUT units. On-demand: $0.040 per GB ingested + $0.040 per GB retrieved. Enhanced Fan-Out: $0.015/shard-hour-consumer. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: On-demand mode has a 5-min scale-up — bursts hit throughput limits before scaling
Extended retention (>24hr) and EFO each add significant cost
Shard count determines fan-in throughput — under-provisioning causes producer throttling

cloudwatch.name: Amazon CloudWatch
cloudwatch.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)
cloudwatch.limits: Metric resolution: 1s (high-res, extra cost) / 60s standard | Log retention: 1 day–10 years (set per log group!) | Dashboard widgets: max 500 | Alarms: max 10 dimensions per metric | Logs Insights: max 20 log groups per query / 15 min query timeout | GetMetricData: max 500 metrics per request
cloudwatch.whenToUse: AWS-native monitoring. Set log retention explicitly — default is never expire (costly). Use metric filters to turn logs into metrics. Alarm on p99 latency, error rate, not just average.
cloudwatch.cost: Metrics: $0.30/metric/mo (first 10k). Logs ingestion: $0.50/GB. Logs storage: $0.03/GB/mo. Insights queries: $0.005/GB scanned. Alarms: $0.10/mo each. Dashboards: $3/mo each. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Default 'never expire' log retention causes silent bills that compound for years — set retention on every log group
Custom metrics at 1s resolution are 4x the cost of 60s — only use for critical latency tracking
Logs Insights queries against terabytes can cost dollars per query — scope time range
Container Insights enabled per task adds per-task metric cost

xray.name: AWS X-Ray
xray.limits: Trace retention: 30 days | Sampling: default 5% + 1 req/s per host | Max segment size: 64 KB | Groups: max 25 per account | Sampling rules: max 25 | Insights: auto-detect anomalies but adds cost | Not available in all regions
xray.whenToUse: Distributed tracing — visualise request flow across services. Best for debugging latency in microservices. Integrate with Lambda, ECS, API Gateway, ALB for end-to-end traces.
xray.cost: Trace recording: $5 per 1M. Trace retrieval/scan: $0.50 per 1M. Insights: $1.00 per 1M traces analyzed. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Default 5% sampling rate is fine for cost but misses rare bugs — adjust per service
Each AWS SDK call instrumented = a subsegment = $$ at scale
Insights adds ML analysis — useful but doubles trace cost
xray.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: 99.9% | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

agentcore.name: AWS AgentCore
agentcore.limits: Concurrent agent invocations: quota-based per account | Context window: bounded by underlying model | Tool call depth: configurable max steps | Memory TTL: session or persistent (configurable) | Supports LangGraph, Strands, custom runtimes
agentcore.whenToUse: AWS-managed agent runtime — handles orchestration, tool calling, memory, observability. Best for multi-agent architectures on Bedrock. Reduces infrastructure overhead vs self-hosted agent frameworks.
agentcore.cost: Pricing is per agent invocation + underlying Bedrock model tokens. Memory and code interpreter charged separately. Check current pricing — service is evolving rapidly. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Tool call loops can compound — set max steps conservatively
Memory storage bills separately — set TTL on session memory
Underlying Bedrock model cost is the dominant line item — pick model deliberately
agentcore.sla: Single AZ: N/A | Single Region: tracks underlying Bedrock SLA (99.9%) — verify current AgentCore SLA at launch | Multi-Region: 99.9999% (calculated)

az-func.name: Azure Functions
az-func.limits: Max execution: 10 min (Consumption) / unlimited (Premium/Dedicated) | Memory: 1.5 GB (Consumption) | Cold start: 1–3s (Consumption) | Max instances: 200 (Consumption) | HTTP trigger timeout: 230s | Durable Functions orchestrator replay limit: watch for large history
az-func.whenToUse: Serverless compute on Azure. Best for event-driven, short-lived tasks. Use Premium plan to eliminate cold starts for latency-sensitive workloads.
az-func.cost: Consumption: $0.20 per 1M executions + $0.000016 per GB-second. Free grant: 1M executions + 400k GB-s/mo. Premium plan: hourly + per-execution. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Consumption cold starts can be 1-3s — use Premium pre-warmed instances for latency SLAs
Durable Functions orchestrator replays the entire history every continuation — keep history small
Premium plan has a base monthly cost even with zero traffic
az-func.sla: 99.95% (Premium / Dedicated) | Consumption: no formal SLA for cold-start latency

az-cosmos.name: Azure Cosmos DB
az-cosmos.limits: Max item size: 2 MB | Max partition key value length: 2 KB | RU/s per partition: 10,000 max | Cross-partition queries are expensive in RUs | Point reads: cheapest (1 RU for 1 KB) | Consistency levels: 5 options — Strong adds 2x RU cost | Max stored procedures size: 256 KB
az-cosmos.whenToUse: Globally distributed multi-model database. Best for globally distributed apps needing low-latency reads/writes. Choose consistency level carefully — Strong consistency doubles cost.
az-cosmos.cost: Provisioned: $0.008/100 RU/s per hour (~$23/mo for 400 RU/s minimum). Serverless: $0.25 per 1M RU consumed. Storage: $0.25/GB/mo. Multi-region writes 2x. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Strong consistency doubles RU cost — only enable per query when truly required
Cross-partition queries can blow through RU budget — design partition key for query patterns
Provisioned min 400 RU/s per container — many small containers = many minimums
Multi-region writes are 2x the storage and RU cost

az-blob.name: Azure Blob Storage
az-blob.limits: Max block size: 4 GB | Max blob size: 190.7 TB | Max IOPS: storage account level (20,000 for standard) | Soft delete retention: up to 365 days | Lifecycle management: up to 100 rules | Access tiers: Hot/Cool/Cold/Archive (Archive retrieval: hours)
az-blob.whenToUse: Azure object storage. Best for unstructured data, backups, media, data lake. Set lifecycle policies to move cold data to Archive tier for cost savings.
az-blob.cost: Hot tier (LRS): $0.0184/GB/mo. Cool: $0.01/GB/mo. Cold: $0.0036/GB/mo. Archive: $0.00099/GB/mo. Operations + egress billed separately. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Egress to internet is a major cost driver — pair with Azure CDN
Early deletion fees apply to Cool/Cold/Archive — design lifecycle policies carefully
Archive retrieval takes hours and has retrieval fees per GB

gcf.name: Google Cloud Functions
gcf.limits: Max execution: 60 min (2nd gen) / 9 min (1st gen) | Memory: 32 GB (2nd gen) | Max concurrent requests: 1,000 per instance (2nd gen) | Cold start: 100ms–2s | Max file size: 100 MB (source) | Event payload: 10 MB
gcf.whenToUse: Serverless functions on GCP. 2nd gen (Cloud Run based) supports longer timeouts and higher concurrency. Best for event-driven workloads triggered by Pub/Sub, GCS, HTTP.
gcf.cost: $0.40 per 1M invocations + $0.0000025 per GB-s + $0.0000100 per GHz-s. Free tier: 2M invocations + 400k GB-s + 200k GHz-s/mo. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: 2nd gen runs on Cloud Run — same cold start behavior, but more flexible
Network egress charged separately — VPC connector adds cost too
Min instances eliminate cold starts but bill 24/7 per instance

bq.name: Google BigQuery
bq.limits: Query timeout: 6hrs | Max results per query: 10 GB (interactive) | Streaming insert: max 1 MB per row / 1 GB per second per project | Max tables per dataset: unlimited (but UI shows 50K) | Slot-based pricing: on-demand = 2,000 slots max | DML (UPDATE/DELETE) counts toward daily limits
bq.whenToUse: Serverless analytical data warehouse. Best for petabyte-scale SQL analytics, BI dashboards. Partitioning + clustering dramatically reduces query cost. Not for OLTP — use Cloud SQL/Spanner.
bq.cost: On-demand queries: $5 per TB scanned. Flat-rate: $20/slot-hour ($2,000/mo for 100 slots). Storage: $0.02/GB/mo active, $0.01/GB/mo long-term (auto after 90 days). (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: SELECT * scans the whole table — always project specific columns
Lack of partitioning turns every query into a full scan — partition on date and cluster on filter columns
Streaming inserts cost $0.01/200 MB on top of storage — batch loads are free
Materialized views save cost on repeated aggregations but storage charges apply

router.name: Router
router.limits: Routing table size depends on hardware model | BGP session limits vary | High CPU on complex ACLs | Single point of failure without redundant pair | Config changes require maintenance window
router.whenToUse: Core network routing between subnets, VLANs, or WAN links. Deploy in redundant pairs for HA. Document all routing policies and ACLs.
router.cost: Hardware + maintenance contract varies by vendor. SD-WAN/cloud equivalents (AWS Transit Gateway: $0.05/hr + $0.02/GB) replace hardware in many setups.
pitfalls: Single router = single point of failure — always pair for HA
BGP misconfigurations can blackhole traffic — peer review changes
Logging at line-rate often saturates collection — sample or filter

firewall.name: Firewall
firewall.limits: Throughput: hardware/license dependent | Stateful inspection adds latency (~1–5ms) | SSL inspection significantly impacts performance | Rule count affects processing time | Log volume can be very high — plan storage
firewall.whenToUse: Network perimeter security. Define strict ingress/egress rules. Enable logging for compliance. Regularly audit rules — unused permissive rules are common security gaps.
firewall.cost: Hardware appliances + license. Cloud equivalents: AWS Network Firewall $0.395/hr + $0.065/GB processed; Azure Firewall ~$1.25/hr.
pitfalls: SSL inspection adds significant CPU — size for peak load
Stale 'allow any' rules from migrations are common attack surface — audit quarterly
Logging traffic at scale needs SIEM sizing — costs more than the firewall

server.name: Server
server.limits: Single point of failure without clustering/HA | Patching requires downtime without rolling update strategy | Local storage lost on hardware failure | Manual scaling — no auto-scale
server.whenToUse: Physical or virtual server. Plan for HA with load balancing and redundant instances. Use configuration management (Ansible/Terraform) for consistent provisioning.
server.cost: Bare-metal: capex + power + cooling + colocation. Cloud VM equivalents: EC2 t3.medium ~$30/mo, Azure D2 ~$70/mo, GCP e2-medium ~$25/mo.
pitfalls: Capacity planning surprises kill bare-metal projects — start with cloud unless you have specific reasons
Patching policies often forgotten — automate via Ansible/Puppet/Salt
Local-only data is your problem — backup discipline is non-negotiable

cylinder.name: Generic Database
cylinder.limits: Connection pool size must match application threads | Index design critical for query performance | Backup retention and RPO/RTO must be defined | Disk I/O is often the bottleneck
cylinder.whenToUse: Relational database. Design schema carefully — refactoring at scale is expensive. Define indexes on all foreign keys and frequent query columns.
cylinder.cost: Varies wildly by deployment. Self-hosted: VM + storage + ops time. Managed (RDS/Cloud SQL/Azure SQL): instance + storage + I/O + backups.
pitfalls: N+1 queries are the #1 performance killer — use eager loading
Missing indexes get expensive as data grows — review slow query log monthly
Backups untested = no backups — practice restore quarterly

queue.name: Generic Queue
queue.limits: Message size limits vary by implementation | At-least-once delivery requires idempotent consumers | FIFO ordering adds throughput constraints | Dead-letter queues needed for failed messages
queue.whenToUse: Async decoupling between services. Producers and consumers scale independently. Always implement DLQ and alerting on DLQ depth.
queue.cost: Self-hosted (RabbitMQ/Kafka): VM + storage + ops. Managed (SQS/Service Bus/Pub-Sub): per-message + retention.
pitfalls: Idempotency is required for at-least-once delivery — design consumers to deduplicate
DLQ without alerting = silent data loss — alert on DLQ depth > 0
Backpressure handling matters more than queue speed

lb.name: Load Balancer
lb.limits: Health check interval affects failover speed | SSL termination offloads CPU from backends | Session affinity can cause uneven load | Max connections depends on instance size
lb.whenToUse: Distributes traffic across multiple backends. Enables zero-downtime deployments. Configure health checks conservatively — too aggressive causes flapping.
lb.cost: AWS ALB ~$16/mo + LCU. Azure Load Balancer ~$22/mo + data. GCP HTTP(S) LB $0.025/hr per forwarding rule + $0.008-$0.012/GB.
pitfalls: Health checks too frequent or strict cause unnecessary failovers
Session affinity (sticky sessions) defeats horizontal scaling — avoid unless required
TCP/UDP needs NLB-style, not ALB — pick the right L4/L7 mix

cache.name: Generic Cache
cache.limits: Memory is finite — eviction policy matters (LRU, LFU) | Cache invalidation is the hardest problem | No persistence by default in most caches | Thundering herd on cache miss at scale
cache.whenToUse: Reduces database load and improves response time. Cache only data that is read frequently and changes infrequently. Set appropriate TTLs to avoid stale data.
cache.cost: Redis on ElastiCache cache.t4g.micro: ~$12/mo. Azure Cache for Redis Basic C0: ~$16/mo. Memorystore basic 1GB: ~$36/mo.
pitfalls: Cache stampedes on miss can take down the DB — use request coalescing or probabilistic early refresh
Stale data bugs are hard to reproduce — TTLs should be a fraction of the data freshness SLA
Cache-aside vs write-through trade-offs matter — pick deliberately

k8s.name: Kubernetes
k8s.limits: Max pods per node: ~110 (default) | etcd size limit: 8 GB | API server rate limiting | Rolling updates require readiness probes | Persistent volumes need storage class planning
k8s.whenToUse: Container orchestration at scale. Enables auto-healing, rolling deployments, horizontal scaling. Significant ops overhead — consider managed K8s (EKS/GKE/AKS) over self-hosted.
k8s.cost: Self-hosted: VM cost + ops time (significant). Managed control plane: EKS $73/mo, GKE Standard $73/mo (Autopilot has different model), AKS free control plane.
pitfalls: Resource requests/limits set wrong = OOM kills or wasted nodes — measure actual usage
etcd is the chokepoint — high churn in CRDs or events can OOM the cluster
Operators add operational complexity — only adopt if you'll actually maintain them
k8s.sla: Varies by provider — GKE regional: 99.95% | AKS: 99.95% (paid SLA) | EKS: 99.9%

az-vm.name: Azure Virtual Machines
az-vm.sla: 99.99% (Availability Zones) | 99.95% (Availability Set, 2+ VMs) | 99.9% (single VM with Premium SSD)
az-vm.limits: Max vCPUs per subscription: 10,000 (default, increasable) | Max NICs per VM: depends on size (up to 8) | Temp disk: ephemeral, lost on reboot | Burstable (B-series): CPU credits exhaust under sustained load | Max data disks: up to 64 (varies by size)
az-vm.whenToUse: Lift-and-shift workloads, licensed software requiring specific OS, GPU workloads (NC/ND series), full OS control. Prefer PaaS (App Service, AKS, Container Apps) for new workloads to reduce management overhead.
az-vm.cost: B2ms (2 vCPU, 8 GB): ~$60/mo. D4s v5 (4 vCPU, 16 GB): ~$140/mo. Reserved instances (1yr) save ~30–40%. Spot VMs save up to 90% but can be evicted. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: "Stopped" VM still charges for compute — must be Stopped (Deallocated) to avoid compute charges
Premium SSD required for 99.9% single-VM SLA — standard SSD gets no SLA
B-series burstable VMs deplete CPU credits under sustained load — CPU-throttled to baseline
Spot VMs can be evicted with 30s notice — design workloads for restartability

az-appservice.name: Azure App Service
az-appservice.sla: 99.95% (Standard / Premium) | Free / Shared tiers: no SLA
az-appservice.limits: Max instances: 30 (Standard), unlimited (Premium v3) | Max apps per plan: 100 | Max deployment slots: 5 (Standard), 20 (Premium) | App size: 50 GB (Standard) | Always On required to prevent app sleep on Basic+
az-appservice.whenToUse: Fully managed PaaS for web apps and REST APIs (.NET, Node, Python, Java, PHP). Zero infra management. Use deployment slots for blue-green deployments. Choose Premium v3 for VNet integration and no cold starts.
az-appservice.cost: B1 (Basic): ~$13/mo. S2 (Standard): ~$73/mo. P2v3 (Premium): ~$155/mo. Consumption (Functions only). Per plan, not per app. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Free / Shared tiers have no SLA and sleep after 20 min of inactivity — not for production
Plan billing is 24/7 regardless of traffic — over-provisioned plans waste money
VNet integration requires Standard or higher; private endpoints require Premium
Deployment slots share the same App Service Plan resources — not a load increase

az-aks.name: Azure Kubernetes Service
az-aks.sla: 99.95% (paid Uptime SLA add-on, Availability Zones) | 99.9% (without paid SLA)
az-aks.limits: 5,000 nodes per cluster | 250 pods per node (default 110) | 1,000 services per cluster | Control plane is free — pay only for agent nodes | Max node pools: 100
az-aks.whenToUse: Managed Kubernetes for containerised microservices at scale. Autopilot mode (pod-based billing, managed nodes) for teams wanting minimal K8s ops. Standard mode for full node control and GPU workloads.
az-aks.cost: Control plane: free. Agent nodes billed as VMs (D2s v5: ~$70/mo/node). 3-node cluster (D2s v5): ~$210/mo. Paid SLA add-on: ~$0.10/cluster/hr. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Paid SLA add-on ($73/mo) required for 99.95% — default free tier is 99.9% only
Node pool upgrades need capacity headroom — plan for surge capacity during upgrades
Egress traffic from pods billed at standard Azure networking rates — costs accumulate
System node pool must remain always-on — cannot scale to zero unlike user pools

az-sql.name: Azure SQL Database
az-sql.sla: 99.99% (Business Critical) | 99.99% (General Purpose with zone redundancy) | 99.9% (General Purpose, single zone)
az-sql.limits: Max DB size: 4 TB (General Purpose) | Max vCores: 80 | Serverless: 0.5–40 vCores auto-scale, pauses after configurable idle | Max concurrent connections: ~30,000 | Hyperscale: up to 100 TB, up to 30 named replicas
az-sql.whenToUse: Fully managed SQL Server on Azure. Choose General Purpose for most workloads. Business Critical for in-memory OLTP and read scale-out. Hyperscale for 4TB+ or unpredictable scale. Serverless for dev/test with intermittent usage.
az-sql.cost: Serverless (0.5 vCore min): ~$5/mo idle. General Purpose 4 vCores: ~$370/mo. Business Critical 4 vCores: ~$1,100/mo. Reserved capacity (1yr) saves ~30%. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Serverless auto-pause causes cold-start connection delays of 60–90s — disable auto-pause for latency-sensitive apps
DTU model caps at 4,000 DTU — migrate to vCore for predictable, scalable pricing
Geo-replication doubles storage cost and adds per-hour replication fee
Connection pool exhaustion common at scale — use Azure SQL connection string with retry logic

az-entra.name: Microsoft Entra ID
az-entra.sla: 99.9% (Entra ID P1 / P2) | 99.9% (Free tier for authentication)
az-entra.limits: Free: 50,000 directory objects | Free B2B: 50,000 monthly active external users | P1 required for Conditional Access, PIM, SSPR | B2C: billed per Monthly Active User (MAU)
az-entra.whenToUse: Identity provider for Azure, M365, and third-party SaaS. Use P1 for Conditional Access and MFA. Use B2C for customer-facing identity (external users). Workload Identity Federation for service-to-service auth without secrets.
az-entra.cost: Free tier: $0. P1: $6/user/mo. P2: $9/user/mo. B2C: first 50k MAU free, then $0.0016/MAU. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Conditional Access requires P1 ($6/user/mo) — not included in free tier
Privileged Identity Management (PIM) requires P2 — budget accordingly for enterprise
B2C MAU billing counts any user who authenticates in that month — spikes are expensive
Guest users (B2B) consume directory object quota — monitor for large partner ecosystems

az-apim.name: Azure API Management
az-apim.sla: 99.95% (Developer with Availability Zones) | 99.99% (Premium multi-region) | Consumption: 99.9% | Developer tier: no SLA (not for production)
az-apim.limits: Consumption: 1M calls/mo included, 3,500 req/s | Standard: 4,000 req/s per unit | Premium: 4,000 req/s per unit | Max policies per API: unlimited | VNet injection: Premium only
az-apim.whenToUse: Full-featured API gateway — rate limiting, caching, auth, transformation, developer portal. Consumption tier for spiky low-volume APIs. Premium for enterprise with VNet, multi-region, and 99.99% SLA.
az-apim.cost: Consumption: $0.035 per 10k calls (first 1M free). Standard: ~$280/mo. Premium: ~$2,800/unit/mo. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Developer tier has no SLA — never deploy to production on Developer tier
VNet integration requires Premium tier ($2,800/unit/mo) — expensive entry point
Self-hosted gateway (for on-premises) requires Premium license — plan ahead
Policy errors (XML misconfiguration) silently break APIs — test policies thoroughly in dev

az-servicebus.name: Azure Service Bus
az-servicebus.limits: Basic: queues only, max 256 KB message | Standard: topics + queues, 80 GB/namespace, max 256 KB | Premium: 1-100 messaging units, max 100 MB message, VNET, zone redundancy | Message lock: 5 min default (max 5 min Basic/Standard, 10 min Premium)
az-servicebus.whenToUse: Enterprise message broker with guaranteed delivery, dead-letter queue, sessions for ordered processing, and FIFO queues. Use topics/subscriptions for pub/sub fanout. Prefer over Storage Queues for enterprise features.
az-servicebus.cost: Basic: $0.05 per 1M operations. Standard: $9.81/mo base + $0.01 per 1M ops. Premium: ~$670/mo per messaging unit. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Basic tier has no topics — only queues; upgrade to Standard for pub/sub
Sessions (for ordered FIFO processing) require Premium tier
Dead-letter queue messages accumulate silently — set up alerts on DLQ depth
Message lock timeout default is short — increase for slow consumers to avoid redelivery

az-eventhubs.name: Azure Event Hubs
az-eventhubs.limits: Basic: 1-day retention, 1 consumer group | Standard: 7-day retention, 20 consumer groups | Premium: 90-day retention, unlimited CGs | 1 TU = 1 MB/s ingress or 1,000 events/s | Max message size: 1 MB (Standard), 1 MB (Premium) | Capture to Blob/ADLS: extra cost
az-eventhubs.whenToUse: High-volume event streaming and telemetry ingestion. Kafka-compatible surface on Standard+. Use for IoT telemetry, clickstreams, log pipelines. Not a message broker — no dead-letter, no ordered delivery guarantees.
az-eventhubs.cost: Basic: ~$10/mo per TU. Standard: ~$22/mo base + per TU. Premium: ~$750/mo per PU. Dedicated: ~$6,000/mo (Capacity Units). (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Event Hubs is a stream store, not a message broker — no DLQ, no retry, no ordered delivery
Capture to Blob Storage adds storage + write operation cost — budget separately
Consumer group limit (20 on Standard) blocks scaling to many independent consumers
Auto-inflate TUs have a cost ceiling to configure — set to avoid runaway bills

az-monitor.name: Azure Monitor
az-monitor.limits: Log Analytics: 500 MB/day free per workspace, then $2.30/GB | Application Insights: 5 GB/month free per resource | Metrics retention: 93 days (free) | Log retention: 30 days included (up to 2 years configurable) | Daily cap configurable to prevent runaway ingestion cost
az-monitor.whenToUse: Full-stack observability for Azure workloads. Application Insights for APM/distributed tracing. Log Analytics for log aggregation and querying. Azure Monitor Metrics for time-series dashboards and autoscaling signals.
az-monitor.cost: Log ingestion: $2.30/GB after 5 GB/day free. Alerts: $0.10 per metric alert/mo. Application Insights: $2.30/GB after free tier. Commitment tiers save up to 30%. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Log ingestion costs surprise teams at scale — always configure a daily ingestion cap
Application Insights sampling helps cost but can lose rare error traces — tune carefully
Verbose dependency tracking (every SQL call, every HTTP call) multiplies log volume fast
Workspace-based Application Insights shares quota with Log Analytics — plan jointly

az-containerapps.name: Azure Container Apps
az-containerapps.limits: Consumption plan: 2M requests + 100k vCPU-s + 250k GB-s free/mo | Max replicas: 300 | Max CPUs: 4 per container | Dedicated plan: reserved capacity, no scale-to-zero | KEDA-based autoscaling | Max scale-out time: depends on workload and KEDA rules
az-containerapps.whenToUse: Serverless containers without Kubernetes complexity. Best for microservices, HTTP APIs, background workers, event-driven workloads. Scale to zero built in. Dapr integration available for service mesh patterns.
az-containerapps.cost: Consumption: $0.000024 per vCPU-s + $0.000003 per GB-s + $0.40 per 1M requests after free tier. Dedicated: ~$0.115/vCPU-hr. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: KEDA scale-from-zero has a cold-start delay — use min-replicas for latency-sensitive apps
Dapr integration adds a sidecar vCPU/memory overhead — factor into cost estimates
Not appropriate for stateful workloads — use AKS with StatefulSets instead
Consumption plan shares capacity — Dedicated plan required for consistent performance SLA

az-openai.name: Azure OpenAI Service
az-openai.limits: Tokens per minute (TPM) quota per deployment — varies by model, region, and subscription tier | Rate limits enforced per subscription per region | Model availability varies by region | Max context: model-dependent (GPT-4o: 128k tokens) | Provisioned throughput units (PTU) for guaranteed capacity
az-openai.whenToUse: Access to OpenAI models (GPT-4o, o1, Whisper, DALL-E, Embeddings) within Azure compliance boundary. Required for data residency, private networking (VNet), and enterprise compliance. Use PTU for predictable throughput at scale.
az-openai.cost: GPT-4o: $2.50/1M input tokens + $10/1M output tokens. GPT-4o mini: $0.15/$0.60 per 1M tokens. Embeddings (ada-002): $0.10/1M tokens. PTU: ~$2/hr per unit. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: TPM quotas are low by default per subscription/region — request increases before production launch
Model availability varies by region — your preferred region may not have the latest model
Latency is higher than OpenAI direct for some regions due to routing through Azure endpoints
PTU (provisioned throughput) is costly but required for predictable performance at enterprise scale

az-keyvault.name: Azure Key Vault
az-keyvault.limits: Max secret/key/certificate size: 25 KB | Max objects per vault: 5,000 (Standard), unlimited (HSM) | RSA 2048 operations: 2,000/10s | Soft-delete: 90-day retention (cannot be disabled) | Purge protection: prevents permanent deletion during retention | Max vaults per subscription: 600
az-keyvault.whenToUse: Centralised secrets management, encryption keys, and certificates for Azure workloads. Use Managed Identity to authenticate — eliminates credential management entirely. Premium tier (HSM-backed) for FIPS 140-2 Level 3 compliance.
az-keyvault.cost: Standard secret operations: $0.03 per 10,000. Certificate renewals: $3/renewal. HSM key operations: $1 per 10,000. Premium (HSM-protected key): $1/key/mo. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Soft-delete cannot be disabled — purge-protected vaults need explicit purge during cleanup
High-volume key operations hit throttling limits — cache secrets locally with short TTL
One vault per environment is an anti-pattern at scale — use separate vaults by workload/team
Certificate near-expiry alerts require explicit configuration — set up alert policies

az-redis.name: Azure Cache for Redis
az-redis.sla: 99.9% (Standard / Premium) | Basic: no SLA (single node, not for production)
az-redis.limits: Basic: 53 MB–53 GB, no replication, no SLA | Standard: primary + replica | Premium: clustering (up to 10 shards), data persistence (RDB/AOF), VNet injection | Max clients per cache: 1,000 (per shard for cluster) | Max memory: 1.2 TB (Enterprise cluster)
az-redis.whenToUse: Distributed cache, session store, pub/sub, leaderboards, rate limiting on Azure. Premium required for VNet injection, persistence, and geo-replication. Enterprise tier for active geo-replication and RediSearch.
az-redis.cost: C1 Standard (1 GB): ~$55/mo. C2 Standard (6 GB): ~$100/mo. P3 Premium (26 GB): ~$440/mo. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Basic tier has no SLA and is single-node — never use in production
Eviction policy not configured by default — set appropriate policy (allkeys-lru for cache, noeviction for session)
Persistence (AOF) in Premium increases storage IOPS cost significantly
Connection multiplexing required (StackExchange.Redis in .NET) — creating connections per request exhausts limits

az-frontdoor.name: Azure Front Door
az-frontdoor.limits: Max origins per profile: 500 | Max endpoints: 10 (Standard), 25 (Premium) | Max custom domains: 500 | WAF policies: 100 managed rules + 100 custom rules | Cache purge: 2 min SLA | Max rules engine rules: 25 per profile
az-frontdoor.whenToUse: Global anycast load balancing, CDN, WAF, and SSL termination in one service. Replaces Traffic Manager + CDN for most workloads. Use Premium for bot protection, Private Link origin connections, and WAF managed rules.
az-frontdoor.cost: Standard: $35/mo base + $0.01/GB data transfer. Premium: $330/mo base. WAF: $5/policy/mo + $0.006/10k requests. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Standard vs Premium have fundamentally different WAF capabilities — Premium required for bot protection
Private Link origins (Premium only) required for secure backend connectivity — plan SKU early
Legacy CDN profiles (Verizon/Akamai/Microsoft Standard) still exist but are being retired
Cache TTL must be set at origin — Front Door respects Cache-Control headers from backend

az-devops.name: Azure DevOps
az-devops.limits: Free: 5 users (Basic), 1 parallel job (1,800 min/mo private), unlimited parallel jobs (public) | Basic: $6/user/mo | Extra parallel jobs: $40/mo (hosted), $15/mo (self-hosted) | Artifacts: 2 GB free, $2/GB/mo extra | Test Plans: $52/user/mo
az-devops.whenToUse: End-to-end DevOps platform (Boards, Repos, Pipelines, Artifacts, Test Plans) best for .NET-heavy or Microsoft-centric shops. Azure Pipelines supports any language and cloud. Use self-hosted agents for large-scale builds or specific environments.
az-devops.cost: Free (5 users): $0. Basic plan: $6/user/mo. Self-hosted agents: free unlimited. Additional parallel jobs: $40/mo. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Parallel job limit of 1 (free) blocks concurrent builds — buy extra jobs or use self-hosted agents
Self-hosted agents are free but require maintenance and scaling management
YAML pipelines have a steep learning curve compared to classic UI pipelines
Service connections (GitHub, Docker Hub etc.) require explicit permission grants per pipeline

gce.name: Google Compute Engine
gce.sla: 99.99% (regional Managed Instance Group) | 99.9% (single VM with premium storage)
gce.limits: Default vCPU quota: 24 per region (increasable) | Max network bandwidth: 100 Gbps | Preemptible/Spot: up to 91% discount, may be reclaimed with 30s notice | Live migration: supported for standard VMs | Custom machine types: any vCPU+memory combination
gce.whenToUse: Full VM control on GCP. GPU instances (A100, H100) for AI training. Custom machine types for memory- or CPU-optimised workloads. Prefer Cloud Run or GKE for containerised stateless workloads.
gce.cost: e2-micro (0.25 vCPU, 1 GB): ~$6/mo. n2-standard-4 (4 vCPU, 16 GB): ~$155/mo. Sustained use discounts up to 30% applied automatically. Committed use (1yr) saves ~37%. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Preemptible/Spot VMs reclaimed with 30s notice — design for restart and use with stateless workloads
Sustained use discounts applied automatically — committed use discounts require explicit purchase
Persistent disk IOPS are provisioned (not burst) — size the disk for IOPS, not just capacity
Egress between regions is charged — architect data flows to minimise cross-region traffic

cloudrun.name: Google Cloud Run
cloudrun.limits: Max instances: 1,000 (default) | Max concurrency: 1,000 req per instance | Max CPU: 8 vCPUs per instance | Max memory: 32 GB | Max timeout: 3,600s | Max request size: 32 MB | Min instances: configurable (eliminates cold start)
cloudrun.whenToUse: Serverless containers on GCP. Best for stateless HTTP services, APIs, microservices that scale to zero. Scale from zero to hundreds of instances in seconds. Full container flexibility without Kubernetes cluster management.
cloudrun.cost: Free: 2M req + 360k vCPU-s + 180k GB-s/mo. Beyond free: $0.24/vCPU-hr + $0.025/GB-hr + $0.40/1M req. Min instances billed 24/7. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: CPU throttled to near-zero between requests by default — enable "CPU always allocated" for background work
Cold starts on first request after scale-to-zero — use min-instances for latency-sensitive APIs
No persistent local disk — all state must go to Cloud Storage or managed databases
Container image stored in Artifact Registry — factor in storage costs for large images

gke.name: Google Kubernetes Engine
gke.limits: 15,000 nodes per cluster | 110 pods per node (configurable up to 256) | 5,000 services per cluster | Autopilot: pod-level billing, managed nodes, min 1 node equivalent | Standard: full node control, scale to zero possible for user node pools
gke.whenToUse: Managed Kubernetes on GCP. Autopilot mode for minimal ops — pay per pod CPU/memory, not per node. Standard mode for GPU workloads, custom node configurations, or privileged containers. Integrated with Anthos for multi-cloud.
gke.cost: Control plane: free (Autopilot) or $0.10/hr (Standard). Autopilot nodes: ~$0.0575/vCPU-hr + $0.006/GB-hr. Standard: node VMs billed as GCE. 3-node n2-std-4 cluster: ~$450/mo. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Autopilot charges minimum pod resource requests — over-requesting CPU/memory wastes budget
Zonal clusters have a single control plane — use regional clusters for production HA
Persistent volumes are zone-scoped by default — cross-zone volume attachment causes pod scheduling issues
Cluster upgrades require testing — use maintenance windows and blue-green node pools

cloudsql.name: Google Cloud SQL
cloudsql.limits: Max storage: 64 TB | Max connections: ~4,000 (db-n1-standard-4) | Max read replicas: 10 | HA: synchronous replication to standby in same region | Cross-region replica: asynchronous read replica, no auto-failover | Cloud SQL Proxy: recommended for secure connections
cloudsql.whenToUse: Fully managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server on GCP. HA with automatic failover for production. Not suited for >64 TB — use AlloyDB or Spanner. Use Cloud SQL Proxy or Workload Identity for secure app connectivity.
cloudsql.cost: db-f1-micro (shared): ~$10/mo. db-n1-standard-2 (HA): ~$140/mo. db-n1-standard-8 (HA): ~$450/mo. Storage: $0.17/GB/mo (SSD). Egress extra. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: HA standby doubles compute cost — required for 99.95% SLA in production
Cross-region read replica adds storage + replication costs and does not auto-failover
Connection limits per instance are fixed — use Cloud SQL Auth Proxy + connection pooler (PgBouncer) at scale
Automatic storage increase cannot be reversed — set max storage limit to avoid cost surprises

pubsub.name: Google Cloud Pub/Sub
pubsub.limits: Max message size: 10 MB | Max subscription retention: 31 days | Max throughput: virtually unlimited (regional) | At-least-once delivery — design consumers to be idempotent | Ordering keys: enables ordering within a key, reduces throughput | Dead-letter topic: configurable max delivery attempts (5–100)
pubsub.whenToUse: Managed async messaging and event streaming on GCP. Best for decoupling microservices, streaming pipelines to Dataflow/BigQuery, fanout to multiple subscribers. Kafka-compatible via Pub/Sub Lite for byte-efficient streaming.
pubsub.cost: First 10 GB/mo: free. $0.04/GB after. Snapshot storage: $0.012/GB/mo. BigQuery subscriptions charged per byte. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: At-least-once delivery means duplicate messages are possible — build idempotent consumers
Default acknowledgement deadline (10s) causes redelivery for slow consumers — increase per subscription
Ordering keys limit throughput per key to one partition — avoid hot keys
Undelivered messages accumulate cost if consumers fall behind — monitor oldest-unacked-message-age

gcs.name: Google Cloud Storage
gcs.limits: Max object size: 5 TB | No bucket size limit | Max metadata per object: 8 KB | Lifecycle rules: 100 per bucket | Object versioning: per-version storage charged | Retention policy: prevents deletion/overwrite for compliance | Uniform bucket-level access: recommended, disables per-object ACLs
gcs.whenToUse: GCP object storage for any scale. Standard for hot data, Nearline ($0.01/GB, 30-day min) for monthly backups, Coldline ($0.004/GB, 90-day) for quarterly, Archive ($0.0012/GB, 365-day) for long-term. Use lifecycle rules to auto-transition.
gcs.cost: Standard (regional): $0.02/GB/mo. Nearline: $0.01/GB/mo. Coldline: $0.004/GB/mo. Archive: $0.0012/GB/mo. Egress: $0.12/GB to internet (first 1 TB/mo). (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Egress to internet is the dominant cost driver — pair with Cloud CDN for public assets
Early deletion fees for Nearline (30d), Coldline (90d), Archive (365d) — design lifecycle policies
Object versioning stores every version at full price — set lifecycle rule to delete non-current versions
XML ACLs on objects are deprecated — migrate to uniform bucket-level IAM access

spanner.name: Google Cloud Spanner
spanner.limits: Processing unit = 0.1 node | 1 node handles ~2,000 QPS reads + 1,000 QPS writes | Max row size: 1024 columns, each up to 2.5 MB | Max database size: unlimited | Max tables: 2,048 per database | No sequential primary keys — use UUID to avoid hot spots
spanner.whenToUse: Globally distributed, strongly consistent, horizontally scalable relational database. For workloads that have outgrown Cloud SQL or need global multi-region writes with ACID guarantees. Expensive — reserve for true global-scale or compliance-critical workloads.
spanner.cost: Processing unit: $0.09/hr (~$65/mo). 1 node: $0.90/hr (~$648/mo). Storage: $0.30/GB/mo. Multi-region configs are 3× the node cost. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Minimum 1 processing unit ($65/mo) — not a dev database, even for tiny workloads
Sequential primary keys create hot spots — always use UUID or hash-prefixed keys
Multi-region config (for 99.999% SLA) costs 3× single-region — validate if required
Interleaved tables improve locality but complicate schema migrations — design upfront

vertexai.name: Google Vertex AI
vertexai.limits: Gemini API: rate limits per project per model per region (TPM/RPM) | Online prediction: latency depends on model size and hardware | Batch prediction: quotas per region | Training: job-level quotas | Model Registry: unlimited models per project | Feature Store: online serving quota per QPS
vertexai.whenToUse: End-to-end ML platform on GCP. Gemini API for foundation model inference. AutoML for no-code training. Custom training for full control with any framework. Model Registry for versioning and deployment. Best for GCP-native ML pipelines.
vertexai.cost: Gemini 1.5 Pro: $3.50/1M input tokens + $10.50/1M output tokens. Gemini Flash: $0.075/$0.30 per 1M tokens. Training: per-hour GPU cost (A100: ~$3/hr). Online prediction: per node-hour. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Prediction endpoints bill per node-hour even at zero traffic — delete idle endpoints
Gemini quota limits per project/region — request increases well before launch
Training job costs can spiral — set max run-time and budget alerts
Feature Store online serving has per-QPS latency SLA — provision adequately for production

cloudarmor.name: Google Cloud Armor
cloudarmor.sla: N/A — WAF and DDoS protection service
cloudarmor.limits: Standard security policies: 40 rules max | Managed Protection Plus: advanced DDoS response, adaptive protection (ML-based), higher rule limits | Rate limiting: configurable per rule | Geo-based blocking: country-level | Preview mode: evaluate rules before enforcing
cloudarmor.whenToUse: WAF and DDoS protection for Cloud Load Balancing and Cloud CDN backends. Managed Protection Plus for guaranteed DDoS response SLA and bot management. Standard for basic OWASP top-10 protection and geo-blocking.
cloudarmor.cost: Standard policy: $5/policy/mo + $1/rule/mo + $0.75/1M requests. Managed Protection Plus: $3,000/mo per project (enterprise DDoS response guarantee). (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Standard Cloud Armor has no DDoS response SLA — Managed Protection Plus required for guaranteed response
Adaptive Protection (ML-based threat detection) requires Managed Protection Plus
WAF rule per-request cost adds up at high traffic — model cost before enabling on high-volume endpoints
Use preview mode to validate rules before enforcing — misconfigured rules can block legitimate traffic

gcpmonitoring.name: Google Cloud Monitoring
gcpmonitoring.limits: Free: 150 MB custom metrics/mo per billing account | Logs: first 50 GB/project/mo free | Trace: first 2.5M spans/mo free | Dashboards: 1,000 per project | Alert policies: 500 per project | Metrics retention: 6 weeks (free), 24 months (configurable)
gcpmonitoring.whenToUse: Full-stack observability for GCP. Cloud Monitoring for metrics and dashboards. Cloud Logging for centralised log aggregation and querying. Cloud Trace for distributed tracing. Cloud Profiler for CPU/memory profiling in production.
gcpmonitoring.cost: Logs ingestion: $0.01/GB (after 50 GB/mo free). Log storage: $0.01/GB/mo (after 30 days). Custom metrics: $0.18/metric-descriptor after free tier. Trace: $0.20/1M spans after free tier. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Log ingestion above 50 GB/mo is $0.01/GB — verbose debug logging at scale creates surprise bills
Exclusion filters reduce ingestion cost — define them early for high-volume log streams
Cloud Trace sampling rate should be tuned — 100% tracing at high QPS is expensive
Log-based metrics incur both log ingestion and metrics charges — double-count in budget

gcplb.name: Google Cloud Load Balancing
gcplb.limits: Global HTTP(S) LB: anycast single IP, 100+ backend services | Forwarding rules: 15 per project (default, increasable) | SSL certificates: 100 per load balancer | Max backends per backend service: 1,000 | Regional LB: zonal scope only
gcplb.whenToUse: Global HTTP(S) LB for worldwide low-latency delivery — traffic enters Google network at nearest PoP. Internal HTTP(S) LB for service-to-service within VPC. Pair with Cloud Armor for WAF and Cloud CDN for edge caching.
gcplb.cost: Forwarding rule: $0.025/hr (~$18/mo). Ingress processed: $0.008/GB. Outbound data from LB: standard network pricing. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Global LB not available in all regions for backends — check regional availability
Internal HTTP(S) LB is regional, not global — a different product from External Global LB
Forwarding rule quota (15/project) blocks fast scaling — request quota increase early
SSL certificate managed by Google rotates automatically — import custom certs for branded domains

cloudbuild.name: Google Cloud Build
cloudbuild.limits: Free: 120 build-minutes/day (n1-standard-1 equivalent) | Max concurrent builds: 10 (default) | Max build timeout: 24 hrs | Machine types: e2-medium to n1-highcpu-32 | Build logs retained 90 days | Build triggers: GitHub, Cloud Source Repos, Bitbucket, GitLab
cloudbuild.whenToUse: Serverless CI/CD on GCP. Trigger builds from source pushes. Native integration with Artifact Registry, Cloud Run, GKE deployments. Use custom build steps for any language or tool. Cloud Deploy for progressive delivery (canary/blue-green).
cloudbuild.cost: First 120 min/day free (n1-standard-1). Standard: $0.003/build-min. High-CPU (n1-highcpu-32): $0.015/build-min. (as of Q2 2026)
pitfalls: Build caches are per-worker and not shared — use Kaniko or remote caching for large Docker builds
Network egress during builds is charged at standard rates — pulling large base images adds cost
Secret Manager integration recommended for build secrets — avoid environment variable secrets in logs
Build step containers are downloaded fresh unless caching configured — increases build time
